title: Representability for Quantum Theory beyond Particle-Number Conservation
abstract: Representability determines when a two-particle reduced density matrix (2-RDM) corresponds to a physical quantum state, enabling many-particle quantum calculations with 2-RDMs rather than the wave function. In this Letter, we present a solution of the representability problem for quantum systems without particle-number conservation. The physically allowed set of 2-RDMs can be characterized from a geometrically `orthogonal' set, the polar cone. We derive explicit linear equations for the two-body operators in the polar cone -- the intersection of the $p$-positive cone with the two-body operator space -- to obtain a systematic hierarchy of representability conditions that do not depend on higher RDMs or the wave function. Moreover, by augmenting these conditions with the particle-number variance, we obtain a unified framework for treating both particle-number-conserving and nonconserving systems. We illustrate with a spin system and molecular H$_4$.
summary: This paper solves the 2-RDM representability problem for quantum systems where particle number is not conserved. By using a polar cone approach, the authors derive a hierarchy of constraints that work for both fixed and fluctuating particle numbers. The (2, 3)-positivity conditions are shown to be highly accurate, even in complex cases like the H4 molecule. This provides a powerful tool for simulating many-body physics without needing the full wave function.
main_problem: The N-representability problem for quantum systems where particle number is not conserved, specifically determining when a 2-RDM corresponds to a physical state in Fock space.
main_result: The development of a systematic hierarchy of (2, p)-positivity conditions that provides a unified framework for both particle-number-conserving and non-conserving systems, with (2, 3)-positivity showing extremely high accuracy.
method: Variational 2-RDM (V2RDM) method using Semidefinite Programming (SDP) and a dual-cone approach to characterize the intersection of the positive cone with the two-body operator space.
model_or_system: The study utilizes a fermionic ring Hamiltonian (a generalization of the BCS Hamiltonian) and the linear H4 molecule to demonstrate the method's applicability to both non-conserving and conserving regimes.
key_observables: Ground-state energy, energy error relative to Full Configuration Interaction (FCI), and particle-number variance.
important_parameters: The interaction parameter kappa/tau in the ring model and the particle-number variance constraint.
main_assumptions: The general N-representability problem is QMA-complete, implying that the full solution has exponential worst-case scaling; the method relies on the (2, p)-positivity hierarchy approximation.
figures_summary: Figure 1 compares ground-state energies and logarithmic errors for various positivity levels in the ring model; Figure 2 shows particle-number variance; Figure 3 shows potential energy curves and energy errors for the H4 molecule dissociation.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the representability problem for non-conserving systems, presents the mathematical derivation of the (2, p)-positivity hierarchy via the polar cone, demonstrates the unified framework using a fermionic ring model, and validates the accuracy using the H4 molecular dissociation.
why_interesting: It provides a new computational framework for studying many-body systems where particle number fluctuates, such as superconducting or superfluid systems, using reduced density matrix methods.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Ground-state energy and (b) base-10 energy error relative to FCI of the six-orbital pairing ring Hamiltonian from V2RDM with (2,2)-positivity, partial (2,3)-positivity, and full (2,3)-positivity.
FIG. 2. Particle-number variance of the ground state of the pairing ring Hamiltonian from V2RDM with (2,2)-positivity, partial (2,3)-positivity, and full (2,3)-positivity, compared with FCI.

Figure 2 shows the particle-number variance as a func- tion of κ/τ from V2RDM with (2, 2)-positivity, par- tial (2, 3)-positivity, and full (2, 3)-positivity, along with FCI. The nonzero variance demonstrates that the ground state of the ring Hamiltonian is number non-conserving. The variance from (2, 2)-positivity becomes too large for κ/τ ≳0.5, while the variance from full (2, 3)-positivity is indistinguishable from FCI for all κ/τ. As in the en- ergy comparison, the partial (2, 3)-positivity results fol- low FCI near equilibrium but depart from the exact re- sults for κ/τ ≈0.5–1.2.
